content:
# Matthew Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_021_page_001.jpg, MAT_021_page_002.jpg

### The Triumphal Entry

1. As they approached Jerusalem and reached Bethphage on the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two disciples ahead.

2. "Go into the village just ahead of you," he said. "You'll find a donkey tied there, with her colt beside her. Untie them and bring them to me.

3. If anyone asks, just say, 'The Lord needs them,' and they will be sent right away."

4. This fulfilled the prophecy:

5. "Tell the people of Jerusalem: 'Look, your king is coming to you — humble, riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.'"

6. The two disciples went and did what Jesus told them.

7. They brought the donkey and the colt, laid their cloaks on them, and Jesus sat on them.

8. A massive crowd spread their cloaks on the road ahead of him, while others cut branches from the trees and scattered them along the path.

9. The crowds walking ahead and behind him were shouting, "Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest heaven!"

10. When Jesus entered Jerusalem, the entire city buzzed with excitement. "Who is this?" everyone asked.

11. The crowds replied, "This is the prophet Jesus, from Nazareth in Galilee."

### Jesus Clears the Temple

12. Jesus went into the temple and threw out everyone who was buying and selling there. He overturned the tables of the money changers and the chairs of those selling doves.

13. "The Scriptures say, 'My house will be called a house of prayer,'" he declared. "But you've turned it into a hideout for thieves!"

14. The blind and the lame came to him in the temple, and he healed them.

15. But when the chief priests and the teachers of the law saw the amazing things he did and heard the children shouting "Hosanna to the Son of David!" in the temple, they were furious.

16. "Do you hear what these children are saying?" they demanded. "Yes," Jesus replied. "Haven't you ever read, 'From the lips of children and infants you have drawn perfect praise'?"

17. Then he left them and went out of the city to Bethany, where he spent the night.

### The Fig Tree Withers

18. Early the next morning, as Jesus was heading back to the city, he was hungry.

19. He noticed a fig tree by the road and went over to it, but found nothing on it except leaves. "May you never bear fruit again!" he said. And the fig tree immediately withered.

20. The disciples were amazed. "How did the fig tree wither so quickly?" they asked.

21. Jesus replied, "I tell you the truth, if you have faith and don't doubt, you could do more than what happened to this fig tree. You could even say to this mountain, 'Be lifted up and thrown into the sea,' and it would happen.

22. If you believe, you will receive whatever you ask for in prayer."

### The Authority of Jesus Challenged

23. When Jesus returned to the temple and began teaching, the chief priests and elders confronted him. "By what authority are you doing all this?" they demanded. "Who gave you this authority?"

24. Jesus replied, "I'll ask you one question first. If you answer, I'll tell you by what authority I act.

25. John's baptism — where did it come from? From heaven or from humans?" They talked it over among themselves: "If we say 'from heaven,' he'll ask, 'Then why didn't you believe him?'

26. But if we say 'from humans,' the crowd will turn on us — they all consider John a prophet."

27. So they answered, "We don't know." And Jesus said, "Then I won't tell you by what authority I do these things either.

### The Parable of the Two Sons

28. But what do you think about this? A man had two sons. He went to the first and said, 'Son, go work in the vineyard today.'

29. The son said, 'No, I won't.' But later he changed his mind and went.

30. The father went to the second son and said the same thing. 'Sure, Dad,' the son replied. But he never went.

31. Which of the two obeyed his father?" "The first," they answered. Jesus said, "I tell you the truth, corrupt tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the kingdom of God ahead of you.

32. John came and showed you the path of righteousness, and you refused to believe him. But the tax collectors and prostitutes believed. And even after you saw that, you still refused to change your minds and believe.

### The Parable of the Evil Tenants

33. Now listen to another parable. A landowner planted a vineyard, built a wall around it, dug a winepress, and constructed a watchtower. Then he leased it to tenant farmers and moved away.

34. When the grape harvest approached, he sent servants to collect his share of the crop.

35. But the tenants grabbed the servants — they beat one, killed another, and stoned a third.

36. He sent a larger group of servants, and the tenants treated them the same way.

37. Finally, the owner sent his son. 'Surely they'll respect my son,' he said.

38. But when the tenants saw the son, they said to each other, 'Here comes the heir! Let's kill him and take his inheritance!'

39. So they grabbed him, dragged him out of the vineyard, and murdered him.

40. Now, when the owner of the vineyard returns, what will he do to those tenants?"

41. "He'll bring those wretches to a wretched end!" they replied. "And he'll lease the vineyard to other tenants who will give him his share at harvest time."

42. Jesus said, "Didn't you ever read this in the Scriptures? 'The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. This is the Lord's doing, and it is an amazing thing to see.'

43. What I mean is this: the kingdom of God will be taken away from you and given to a people who will produce its fruit.

44. Anyone who stumbles over this stone will be broken, and anyone it falls on will be crushed."

45. When the chief priests and Pharisees heard his parables, they realized he was talking about them.

46. They wanted to arrest him, but they were afraid of the crowds, who considered Jesus a prophet.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 5 — The humble king:** Jesus' entry into Jerusalem is a deliberate anti-triumph. Conquerors rode warhorses; Jesus rides a donkey. The quotation from Zechariah 9:9 presents a king who comes in peace, not war. The crowd gets the "Son of David" part right — but the kind of king he is will not match their expectations.

**Verse 12 — Clearing the temple:** This is not random anger. The money changers and dove sellers operated in the Court of the Gentiles — the only place where non-Jews could pray. By turning this space into a marketplace, the temple leadership had excluded the nations from worship. Jesus quotes Isaiah 56:7 ("my house shall be called a house of prayer") — and in the original, Isaiah completes the sentence: "for all nations."

**Verse 19 — The fig tree:** This is an acted parable, not a random miracle. The fig tree represents Israel (a common OT image — Jeremiah 8:13, Hosea 9:10). Leaves but no fruit = the appearance of faithfulness without the substance. It's the same charge leveled at the temple: all the religious machinery, none of the justice and mercy God requires.

**Verses 33-46 — The tenants:** The vineyard = Israel (see Isaiah 5). The tenants = the leaders. The servants = the prophets. The son = Jesus. The parable is a capsule history of Israel's relationship with God — and a prediction of what is about to happen to Jesus. The leaders understand perfectly (v.45) but proceed anyway.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
